questions.prompt: A rectangular pulse of width T can be written as:
questions.explanation: u(t) turns on at t=0. u(t‚ąíT) turns on at t=T. Their difference u(t)‚ąíu(t‚ąíT) is 1 for 0‚Č§t<T and 0 elsewhere ‚ÄĒ a rectangular pulse of width T.

questions.prompt: The Laplace transform of u(t‚ąíT) is:
questions.explanation: Time-shift property: ‚Ąí{f(t‚ąíT)u(t‚ąíT)} = e^(‚ąísT)F(s). For u(t): F(s)=1/s. So ‚Ąí{u(t‚ąíT)} = e^(‚ąísT)/s. The e^(‚ąísT) factor represents the delay.

questions.prompt: X(s) for a rectangular pulse u(t)‚ąíu(t‚ąí1) is:
questions.explanation: X(s) = ‚Ąí{u(t)} ‚ąí ‚Ąí{u(t‚ąí1)} = 1/s ‚ąí e^(‚ąís)/s = (1‚ąíe^(‚ąís))/s. The (1‚ąíe^(‚ąís)) factor represents the finite width of the pulse.

questions.prompt: The time-shift property of the Laplace transform states:
questions.explanation: ‚Ąí{f(t‚ąíT)u(t‚ąíT)} = e^(‚ąísT)F(s). Note: the u(t‚ąíT) is essential ‚ÄĒ it ensures the signal is zero for t<T (causal shift). The e^(‚ąísT) multiplier in s-domain corresponds to a T-second delay in time.

questions.prompt: The pulse response of an LTI system equals:
options: Step response minus delayed step response
questions.correctAnswer: Step response minus delayed step response
questions.explanation: Since x(t) = u(t)‚ąíu(t‚ąíT), by linearity: y(t) = y_step(t) ‚ąí y_step(t‚ąíT). The pulse response is the difference of two step responses, separated by delay T. Superposition at work!

questions.prompt: In the s-domain, a delay of T seconds is represented by:
questions.explanation: e^(‚ąísT) is the Laplace-domain representation of a pure delay. If Y_undelayed(s) is the transform of the undelayed signal, then e^(‚ąísT)¬∑Y(s) is the transform of the signal delayed by T seconds.

questions.prompt: Superposition principle for LTI systems means:
questions.explanation: If input = x‚āĀ+x‚āā, then output = y‚āĀ+y‚āā. This is linearity. It's what allows us to decompose complex inputs into simpler parts, compute each response, and sum. Without superposition, this approach fails entirely.

questions.prompt: For RC pulse response, when 0 ‚Č§ t < 1 (pulse still on):
questions.explanation: During the pulse (0‚Č§t<1), only the first step response is active. The second step response hasn't 'turned on' yet (it activates at t=1). So y(t) = 1‚ąíe^(‚ąít/RC), identical to the step response.

questions.prompt: For RC pulse response, when t ‚Č• 1 (pulse off):
questions.explanation: After t=1, both step responses are active. y = first step ‚ąí second step = (1‚ąíe^(‚ąít/RC)) ‚ąí (1‚ąíe^(‚ąí(t‚ąí1)/RC)) = e^(‚ąí(t‚ąí1)/RC) ‚ąí e^(‚ąít/RC). The capacitor discharges back to 0.

questions.prompt: What is the final value of the pulse response y(‚ąě)?
questions.explanation: After the pulse ends, the input is zero and the capacitor discharges fully. y(‚ąě)=0. Both exponentials decay to zero as t‚Üí‚ąě. The system returns to rest after any finite-duration input.

questions.prompt: A staircase signal (step at t=0, step at t=1, step at t=2) can be written as:
questions.explanation: Each step adds 1 at its onset time. u(t)+u(t‚ąí1)+u(t‚ąí2) gives: 0 for t<0, 1 for 0‚Č§t<1, 2 for 1‚Č§t<2, 3 for t‚Č•2. By superposition, the output is the sum of three step responses with delays.

questions.prompt: The key advantage of the superposition approach is:
options: Complex inputs can be handled using already-known simple responses
questions.correctAnswer: Complex inputs can be handled using already-known simple responses
questions.explanation: Once you've computed the step response, you can reuse it for pulse, staircase, and other composed inputs ‚ÄĒ just shift and scale. This saves enormous computation compared to starting from scratch each time.
